chunks.raw_text:

TABARAK: So fun stuff.

MIRAQUE: It's great. But thanks for taking the time.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text:
TABARAK: Yeah, thanks for taking the time. I really appreciate you just making time for a new founder. And like I was saying in the message, we're in the super early stages of product development right now. And so what I wanted to do was just go through like a couple problem statements that we have and see if these are things that you feel on a day to day or like week to week basis. And because we're in such an early stage, like if you say no, that's not even close. I don't feel any of that. I'm not going to get offended or anything. But yeah, if the product doesn't make any sense, no big deal. But if it does sound like something that maybe would help make life easier between like juggling clients and your personal time, I would love to potentially set up like next steps and show you what it looks like.

MIRAQUE: Sure.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text:
TABARAK: Okay, awesome. Just a little bit about myself. So I come from a tech consulting background. And I actually I left my job last year. I worked at DocuSign for a very long time. And I quit and I moved to Bangkok and started to start my own business about a year ago. And yeah, it's a big, big, big shift in my life. And I got into consulting work. But one of the things I realized is I, I was more hands on. And I found myself doing a lot of the work I couldn't just advise. And so I was doing more fractional type of work. And I got to about three clients. And what started happening for me is I kind of like you're talking about the six calendars is I just had a lot to juggle. And I was having a really hard time managing my own time and then managing other people's businesses. And that's where this product came from was basically from from those problems. So I did a little bit about myself.

MIRAQUE: Okay.

TABARAK: I'm gonna share my screen. And you said it's pronounced Merak, right?

MIRAQUE: Yep. That's right. It's like Barak, Merak.

TABARAK: I literally, I literally wrote that down. That's gonna be the easiest way for me to remember it.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text:
TABARAK: Okay, so here I have a just a list of problems that we're trying to solve for. If you could just like look through the list and let me know, do any of these resonate with you?

MIRAQUE: Uh-huh. Yeah. Okay. A little bit less of the saying no to new opportunities, but yes, urgent blue work will work blows up your week. So you keep busy, but don't move forward on your bigger business goals. Correct. Stitching together multiple paid tools into some commitments. Yep. Losing client trust because you have no system for protecting commitments when shit hits the fan midweek. Uh-huh. Yeah. Oh, yeah. Lots of this is resonating.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text:
TABARAK: How would you, how would you rank these? Like what, from the worst to you said, you said the saying no to new opportunities, not really high on the list of what would be like probably the top, top three.

MIRAQUE: I would definitely say losing client trust because it's like the biggest risk. It's the thing you don't want to happen. It doesn't happen frequently. What does happen more frequently for me personally is feeling overwhelmed or anxious because I'm constantly worried about letting myself or my clients down. Um, cause the work that they need done is a lot. And, um, people problems are only people problems when they're really problems. Right.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: MIRAQUE: Um, it is calm until like something blows up. Right. When that blows up, that can take a lot of time. And if I'm doing that and building out somebody else's tool, sometimes it's just the balancing of the two is a lot to do. And then, um, the like, uh, urgent week blowing up midweek, like not, not tracking towards your bigger goals. Cause you're focused on your, your weekly goals and then stitching the multiple tools together. The either of those. Yeah. Those are pretty, those resonate as well because I'm, I'm using so many tools on the clients.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text:
MIRAQUE: Like, um, I use tools for my client. Then I use tools for my business and sometimes there's overlap. Sometimes there's not. And there's also like a CRM and other tools that I'm using to attract and maintain client relationships, but also like sales and everything else to be able to get clients. There's a lot of tools. Like I spend all of my time in some kind of tool.

TABARAK: I hear that.

MIRAQUE: And when you sum it up that way, yes, it's like all of my time. And then some of them are replicated, right? So like I have my own HRS system. I have my own, um, ATS system and then I'm in their ATS system and their HRS. Like it's a lot of, a lot of stuff.
chunks.adoption_barrier: duplicate systems across own business and clients

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text:
TABARAK: Yeah. Well this next one, I have like, um, the current solutions, uh, to these problems that a lot of people have. So under tools, like I have Notion, Asana, Trello. Um, so I, I guess just kind of going through these solutions. So starting with the one, um, you, you said was probably the biggest one. Um, just spending a little bit of time each day trying to figure out like, what are my priorities today?

MIRAQUE: Yeah. I don't know.

TABARAK: What is your solution to make sure you're not losing client trust right now?

MIRAQUE: Um, so I have relaxed or loosened my expectations in terms of timelines with clients. So if I know, like for an example, I can build out an applicant tracking system in two weeks. I don't promise it in two weeks, right? Because I don't know what's going to happen in two weeks worth of time. So I don't promise I can get it done in two weeks. If one that's not standard and two, they're not requiring me to rush. So oftentimes I am the person putting these ridiculous timelines and expectations on myself because I want to get projects done completely quickly. Um, so that's something that I've stopped doing. So I've relaxed my timelines a bit based off of when they actually need it. Um, so managing how much I'm committing on the front end is something that I've done.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: Um, something else is I've changed my communication style with clients. So they expect to hear from me once to twice per week. And if there are days in between where they don't hear anything from me, it doesn't mean something is on fire. It just means that I don't have anything else to share between updates. And if I do, I will reach out. Um, so managing expectations on communication has also been something I've done.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: Um, something else I've been doing is automating as much as possible. If I can automate it, if Chad can do it, if Claude can build it, if I can, can scribe, make the SLP, if something else could you be used to free up more of my time, I've done that. And as much automation as possible. So that way they get what they need when they're supposed to. And it's not a manual process for me has been something I've been able to do to protect my time.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: And then also like, if it's not a fire, please don't contact me before 10 AM. Like, I don't want to talk to you.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: MIRAQUE: Time, right? Like it's the same email you don't need to, I can schedule. So like I've scheduled that automated, that done a lot of things like that, um, scheduled invoicing to make sure that that's not manual. So that it's automated. It gets triggered off of specific timelines based off of projects. Um, or like if I send out a contract with an agreement, they could pick their option, pay the invoice. I don't have to worry about it. Those are things that I'm now building and have built into my process. So I'm not spending the manual time and I can just sit down in the mornings and like get a clear lay of the land.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: MIRAQUE: Um, for tool stacking, I have stopped using Notion, Asama, Trello, spreadsheets. Um, I do use 90 million calendars, but I'm not syncing between all of the tools anymore. I look at like as many calendars in one location as I possibly can. Um, so I can see where I am, where I'm supposed to be or what's happening. Um, I stopped using Notion, Asama, and Trello for tracking tasks. Um, and stuck with one source of truth, right? Like if I don't have one source of truth, I will find myself spending a bunch of manual hours, making sure everything is aligned. And that's just a waste of my time, right?
chunks.adoption_barrier: syncing between multiple tools wastes manual hours

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: MIRAQUE: Um, so based off of that, I've, I've actually turned my CRM into my, um, project management tool. So I can see what's happening, where I am, what I need to get done, et cetera, there. It's doing it anywhere else because if I'm in 16 different projects, I mean, and I've used them all. I have a Notion, I have a Trello, I have an Asama, I have a ClickUp, I have a, you know, I did the spreadsheet, like I have tried it all. And if I have to manually update, move things, click things, shift things, it just stops being helpful for me.
chunks.adoption_barrier: manual updating across tools makes them stop being useful

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text:
MIRAQUE: Um, and it doesn't like what they're not, there's one, there is no one perfect tool, right? Like ClickUp is pretty good though.

TABARAK: Yeah. It's a pretty good job, right?

MIRAQUE: ClickUp is, is all of those tools had a baby, right? If Notion, Asama, Trello, spreadsheets, a calendar and Slack had a baby, that baby would be ClickUp. But they all morphed together. So ClickUp is pretty good, but I just, I had to step away from it because going like the docs are here, but the times are here, but the Kanban board is here, but the overview is here and the calendars are over here. Like I had to pull out, right? Um, especially because I understand that my business model means I'm going to be adding another calendar every time I pick up another client. Right. Um, had to stop.
chunks.adoption_barrier: too fragmented across views; new calendar per client breaks the model

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text:
TABARAK: Yeah. Do you have like a Kanban board in your CRM or like, uh, is there time management tools in there?

MIRAQUE: I use base 24. Okay. Sorry. Base 22. Base 22 I think is what it's called to build out an app that lays over my CRM that allows me to project manage what's going on. That's how I made it.

TABARAK: And does your CRM plug into your calendars or are you still, those are just separate still?

MIRAQUE: It does. It plugs into most of my calendars, but because I don't, okay. So if I don't have admin access to the calendar, then I can't add it to my CRM. So for an example, um, let's say when you're ready to hire team members and I come to work with you, you would be setting me up with my own email instance for your company. And if I'm not the admin workspace, I can't then pull you into my CRM. Right. I, so because of that, I keep those separate. Um, and in those instances, I keep my business calendar as my one source of truth and send out my availability based off of that calendar versus your instance in comparison to another client's instance. Cause otherwise it's a hot mess.
chunks.adoption_barrier: admin access required to sync client calendars into CRM

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text:
TABARAK: I see. Okay. Okay. And do you, do you build these things yourself?

MIRAQUE: So the CRM, um, no, I did not build the CRM is go high level, but all of the funnels and workflows and automations and things like that I built inside of the platform myself. Um, and then, um, I used, I want to make sure base 44, it is 44 base 44 to build out the, um, task management.

TABARAK: Oh, okay. Oh, you're, you're, you're techie to that.

MIRAQUE: Um, just a sprinkle, just a sprinkle, just impressive.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text: TABARAK: Um, I had the amount of automation that you're doing. I think, that's, that's how, that's how I like to live my life too. And then, and then as far as like the, um, getting locked, like focusing on the weeks and days and kind of losing sight of the months and years, like what, what do you, what do you do about that? Like the bigger goal planning.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: MIRAQUE: So that has been a struggle. Um, what I have tried doing as in tried, as in this quarter is first quarter, we are trying this, um, is I'm setting time once a month on the calendar to look at the quarter. So I will block off the time, two hours a day for a week to say that these two hours, I'm not looking at anything, no day-to-day tasks, no minute tasks, no, what needs to happen right now. I need to be thinking about strategy, long-term direction next quarter. What are we doing? How are we, how are we trending against the goal for the year? That's when I do it. I pick one week of every month and I take two hours a day to focus on just that. And during that two hours, I'm not available to anybody. Um, yeah.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text:
TABARAK: How's that going? Do you, do you ever skip it or,

MIRAQUE: um, I haven't skipped it yet. So I've been keeping pretty firm on it. Um, it is also Q1. Ask me this in July.

TABARAK: That's what I had to do. And I'm not judging. I literally, we, uh, we, we always end up kicking the can down the road. So that's the only reason I asked that question because I know I'm guilty of that.

MIRAQUE: I understand. I'm pretty good, but we'll see. Ask me in two quarters and we'll see how.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text: TABARAK: Okay. So, so the last thing I wanted to show you, but it's kind of like, it is a high level overview of what our solution is to the problem. Um, and, and I know you, you, uh, have tried every single tool. So, um, you probably heard a lot of promises out there. So I'll go through what are, I do think we have a little bit of a different approach, but, um, what we're building is the AI system that plans your week and reprioritize instantly when fire drills hit. So you can stay focused on what matters most. So, um, I think you called click up, uh, all those tools with, uh, if they had a baby, right. Um, so we're, we're aiming for something, uh, similar in terms of like, um, combining the best of all the worlds. Um, but I think one of the big ones from just hearing on this call is like being able to pull everything from your other tools into one place, like, um, the, the calendar and, and, and all that. Um, and then, and then like, you know, there's a combine board to manage your week.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text: TABARAK: But I think the, the biggest difference that we have is being able to take a task, break it down and then actually measure, um, the estimate of what the effort is and then what you're taking on within a week. Because I know for me personally, um, when I started adopting scrum for my own life, but the biggest thing I realized is I always felt guilty because I felt like I wasn't doing enough. But then when I actually mapped it out, I was like, Oh, I'm doing too much actually. And I didn't have a really good understanding of my capacity. And so, um, what, what we're doing is we're tracking, okay, well, how long does it actually take you to get this thing done? So that the next time you go come around and do it, you're actually planning your capacity accordingly. So you're not over committing and that's kind of our solution to that. And then, and then understanding your, your longer term goals and making sure that you're, you are driving towards those. So, um, not just keeping you productive in the week, which I think to do list often do is they're more worried about your day or your week. Um, but, but being able to use AI to step back and actually like understand, okay, well, are your, are your days and weeks moving towards your months and
